items.title: Trump signs executive order reclassifying federal workforce positions
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order reclassifying roughly 8,000 senior policy-influencing federal positions into a new Schedule Policy/Career category.
items.details: The order makes the affected positions at-will, allowing agencies to remove employees for performance or misconduct without certain procedural steps. The administration states the roles remain career positions with non-partisan hiring processes, and that removal decisions are to be made without respect to political affiliation.

items.title: Executive order issued on advanced AI innovation and security
items.summary: The White House issued an executive order titled 'Promoting Advanced Artificial Intelligence Innovation and Security' on June 2, 2026.
items.details: The order directs federal agencies to strengthen government and private-sector cyber defenses related to advanced AI and to develop benchmarking and review frameworks for secure development of frontier AI models. It assigns tasks to agencies including the NSA, CISA, and the Department of Commerce.

items.title: Executive order directs customs enforcement reforms
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order directing the Department of Homeland Security and Customs and Border Protection to strengthen customs enforcement requirements.
items.details: The order calls for increased bonding requirements for importers of record, heightened entry requirements for foreign importers, and new disclosure and certification rules. The administration says the changes will be implemented through the standard rulemaking process and will not take effect immediately.

items.title: U.S. and Iran sign memorandum of understanding to end war
items.summary: The presidents of the United States and Iran signed a memorandum of understanding on June 17 establishing a 60-day extension of a ceasefire to negotiate final terms.
items.details: The U.S.-Iran agreement, reached earlier in the week, calls for hostilities to end on all fronts, the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, and 60 days of bilateral negotiations. Following the interim deal, the U.S. lifted its blockade of Iran's ports.

items.title: Israel and Hezbollah agree to renewed ceasefire in Lebanon
items.summary: Israel and Hezbollah agreed to a ceasefire on June 19 after escalated fighting in Lebanon threatened U.S.-Iran talks in Switzerland.
items.details: President Trump said he asked Israel to agree to the ceasefire after deadly Israeli strikes in Lebanon. Both sides accused each other of violations, and Iran balked at beginning talks with Vice President Vance over the Lebanon fighting.

items.title: Vance meets mediators in Switzerland for Iran talks
items.summary: Vice President JD Vance met with Pakistani and other officials in Switzerland on June 21 ahead of high-level talks aimed at advancing a deal to end the Middle East conflict.
items.details: Delegations from the United States and Iran, along with mediators from Pakistan and Qatar, gathered at the Bürgenstock resort near Lucerne. Vance said progress was being made and sought to downplay continued Israeli-Hezbollah fighting in Lebanon.

items.title: Senate passes immigration enforcement funding bill
items.summary: The Senate voted 52-47 to pass roughly $70 billion in legislation funding Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol through the end of the presidential term.
items.details: The vote came early on June 5 after weeks of delays and disputes over an unrelated settlement fund. Senators defeated multiple amendments, including efforts by some members to block payouts from the settlement fund.

items.title: House passes Secure America Act
items.summary: The House passed S. 2, the Secure America Act, on June 9 by a simple majority on a passage vote.
items.details: The bill had previously passed the Senate on June 5. The House vote was recorded as House Vote #214 in 2026.

items.title: Senate advances 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act
items.summary: The Senate made progress on a major housing bill, H.R. 6644, the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, which has moved between chambers.
items.details: The Senate invoked cloture by a vote of 84-8 on a motion related to the measure and was scheduled to vote on adoption of a motion to concur with an amendment. The chamber planned to resume consideration of the House message on June 22.

items.title: Supreme Court clears Alabama to use 2023 congressional map
items.summary: The U.S. Supreme Court cleared the way for Alabama to use its preferred congressional map for the 2026 midterms.
items.details: The state will use a map originally drawn in 2023 that a federal court had previously barred from use for discriminating against Black voters. Reporting indicates the map is likely to reduce the number of Democratic-held House seats in the state from two to one.

items.title: Supreme Court narrows Voting Rights Act in Louisiana v. Callais
items.summary: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Louisiana v. Callais, narrowing the application of Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act.
items.details: Justice Samuel Alito wrote the majority opinion and Justice Elena Kagan wrote the dissent. The Court held that a state runs afoul of Section 2 only if it intentionally drew districts to afford minority voters less opportunity because of race; the ruling drew differing reactions from the two major parties.

items.title: Supreme Court enters final stretch of term with major cases pending
items.summary: The Supreme Court has 22 cases remaining and aims to conclude its work by late June or early July, with opinions next scheduled for June 23.
items.details: Pending cases include Trump v. Barbara on the birthright citizenship executive order, Trump v. Cook involving a Federal Reserve board member, and disputes over presidential authority to remove independent agency leaders. The Court had already issued decisions striking the tariff program and narrowing the Voting Rights Act.

items.title: Georgia Republicans split on Trump-endorsed candidates in runoff
items.summary: In Georgia's June 16 primary runoff, Republicans nominated Trump-endorsed Rep. Mike Collins for U.S. Senate but chose Rick Jackson over the president's preferred candidate for governor.
items.details: Collins will face Democratic incumbent Sen. Jon Ossoff in November, a race watched nationally for Senate control. Jackson, a healthcare executive, defeated Lt. Gov. Burt Jones, who was endorsed by Gov. Brian Kemp.

items.title: New York holds primaries with progressive-centrist contests
items.summary: New York held primary elections on June 23, with Mayor Zohran Mamdani campaigning for a slate of progressive congressional candidates.
items.details: Mamdani and Sen. Bernie Sanders rallied for candidates including former NYC comptroller Brad Lander, who challenged Rep. Daniel Goldman, and Darializa Avila Chevalier, who ran against Rep. Adriano Espaillat. Early voting saw more than 92,000 ballots cast in the first six days.

items.title: States continue mid-decade redistricting ahead of midterms
items.summary: After 10 states enacted new congressional maps in the 2026 cycle, reporting indicates Republicans are positioned to gain up to 16 seats and Democrats up to six.
items.details: Texas, Missouri, North Carolina, Ohio, California, and Utah are among states with new maps, while litigation continues in states including Louisiana. Georgia and Mississippi Republicans, and New York and other Democratic-led states, are also considering further map changes ahead of 2028.

CA.title: Newsom signs election protection law SB 73
CA.summary: Governor Gavin Newsom signed Senate Bill 73, legislation strengthening California's election safeguards against interference and intimidation.
CA.details: The law requires the state Department of Justice to issue guidance to county election officials on responding to law enforcement requests near ballot areas, and establishes criminal penalties for knowingly taking voted ballots from election officials, punishable by a fine, imprisonment, or both.
CA.source: Office of the Governor of California

GA.title: Kemp calls special session on redistricting and elections
GA.summary: Governor Brian Kemp called a special session of the Georgia General Assembly beginning June 17 to address redistricting and election issues.
GA.details: The General Assembly meets in regular session for up to 40 days each year, but special sessions called by the governor are limited in scope to the listed issues. The session was set to take up congressional maps and changes to voting procedures.

VA.title: Spanberger signs gun violence prevention bills
VA.summary: Governor Abigail Spanberger signed more than twenty gun violence prevention bills into law at the Virginia State Capitol on June 17.
VA.details: The new laws include strengthened safe storage requirements, a ban on ghost guns, expanded firearm relinquishment for domestic violence misdemeanors, and changes to the Substantial Risk Order process. A separate measure banning the sale of certain semi-automatic firearms and magazines over 15 rounds takes effect July 1.
VA.source: Johns Hopkins Center for Gun Violence Solutions

OH.title: DeWine signs several bills into law
OH.summary: Ohio Governor Mike DeWine signed several bills into law on June 18, including the 'Keep Them Safe Act' and a measure creating the 'Joshua Alert.'
OH.details: Among the bills were Senate Bill 273 enacting the Keep Them Safe Act with an appropriation, and House Bill 359, which clarifies the statewide emergency alert system for missing autistic or developmentally disabled children. Other bills addressed construction contracts and unemployment compensation law.

NC.title: Stein signs property tax moratorium bill
NC.summary: Governor Josh Stein signed Senate Bill 889 into law, placing a moratorium on property tax increases in North Carolina.
NC.details: The law pauses the use of updated assessed values until January 1, 2027, keeping property taxes at previous levels. Property owners would have until December 31, 2026, to appeal their property values.

MO.title: Kehoe signs public safety bill
MO.summary: Governor Mike Kehoe signed House Bill 2637, a public safety measure, into law on June 12, effective immediately under an emergency clause.
MO.details: The law tightens restrictions on drones, expands venue protections, and creates new offenses including cyberharassment and cyberstalking. It also restructures the state's sex offender registry into a three-tier classification system aligned with federal standards.

NH.title: Ayotte signs 18 bills, vetoes others
NH.summary: Governor Kelly Ayotte signed 18 bills into law during the week of June 12 and issued several vetoes.
NH.details: Signed measures included bills on local school district authority over federal grants, the party filing period, and a quarterly fiscal year budget report requirement. Ayotte vetoed House Bill 1072 on Department of Labor authority and Senate Bill 468 related to marijuana cultivation.

MI.title: NRA files suit over Michigan firearm licensing law
MI.summary: The National Rifle Association and several groups filed a lawsuit on June 15 challenging Michigan's firearm license-to-purchase and registration requirements.
MI.details: The plaintiffs include the Michigan Coalition for Responsible Gun Owners, Michigan Gun Owners, Michigan Open Carry, and four NRA members. The suit challenges the state's license-to-purchase and registration regime.

TX.title: Abbott renews border security disaster proclamation
TX.summary: Governor Greg Abbott renewed the state's border security disaster proclamation on June 16, 2026.
TX.details: The proclamation continues a state of disaster first declared in May 2021 for a number of Texas counties affected by unlawful border crossings. Abbott has amended the proclamation in subsequent renewals to modify the list of affected counties.

DC.title: Lewis George wins Democratic mayoral primary
DC.summary: Council member Janeese Lewis George won the Democratic primary for mayor of Washington, D.C., a result that essentially guarantees her election in November.
DC.details: Voters in the District cast ballots in mid-June primaries. The outcome was noted among progressive Democratic victories in mayoral contests this cycle.

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable events from cited sources and does not endorse any party, candidate, or position.
